danger_signals.visual_form: player pieces rendered in contrasting colors on game board
danger_signals.meaning: visual distinction between competing players; threat posed by opponent-controlled tiles

danger_signals.visual_form: opponent pieces placed adjacent to player's pieces
danger_signals.meaning: imminent threat of piece conversion/capture on next turn

safety_signals.visual_form: player pieces grouped together without adjacent opponent pieces
safety_signals.meaning: territorial security; pieces protected from immediate conversion

safety_signals.visual_form: contiguous regions of same-colored pieces occupying board quadrants
safety_signals.meaning: player dominance in specific areas; strategic advantage

faction_indicators.color_identity: specific NES palette color (likely red or blue)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Spot character animated in player's color
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Spot performs different animations when moving opposing pieces
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: 7x7 game board grid with designated player starting positions

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Spot character rendered in this player's color
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: animation set differs from Player 1's piece movements

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Spot character in third faction color
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: available in NES and DOS versions supporting 4 players

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Spot character in fourth faction color
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: available in NES and DOS versions supporting 4 players

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: scattered colored pieces distributed across 7x7 board; multiple empty tiles visible; balanced color distribution
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: players select pieces to expand outward; Spot performs varied movement animations (dance, roller skate, cartwheel) over medium distances

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: larger contiguous clusters of player colors; fewer empty board spaces; some colors dominating specific quadrants
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: piece placement increasingly constrained to adjacent or near-adjacent positions; Spot animations shift to shorter-range moves

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: board heavily saturated with one or two colors; minimal empty tiles; single color occupying majority of visible board space
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: final moves result in cascade piece conversions; Spot animations performed on remaining few playable positions

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: cursor or highlight indicator on current player's pieces; temporary board state snapshot
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: controller input accepted; piece selection enabled; Spot animation triggers on selected move

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: board state frozen; no active piece highlighting; visual focus on opponent's pieces
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: input blocked for current player; control passed to next player in rotation

environmental_storytelling.example: 7x7 grid layout communicates balanced competition framework; unavailable board locations in certain variations signal rule constraints and asymmetrical territory
continuous colored piece regions tell story of each player's territorial expansion and strategic decisions across game progression

environmental_storytelling.visual_manifestation: adjacent pieces changing color after player move communicates game mechanic consequence and immediate strategic impact

environmental_storytelling.example: Spot performs different movement styles (dance, roller skate, cartwheel, dive, fall backwards) based on move distance and direction, providing visual feedback on move execution and piece manipulation

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: creates new piece at destination; Spot performs short-range animation (dance or skate)
translates existing piece to new location; Spot performs medium-range animation (cartwheel or flip)
all adjacent pieces convert to player's color; Spot animation accompanies conversion cascade
automated move selection visible as CPU-designated pieces animate; board updates without manual controller input
surrounded by opponent pieces on multiple sides; next turn conversion likely unless player moves to block
isolated from opponent pieces; placement options available without immediate counter-threat

notes: Spot: The Video Game is a Reversi/Othello variant using 7 Up mascot 'Spot' as animated piece representation. Visual communication relies primarily on color differentiation between players and Spot animation variety to convey game state, move execution, and territorial changes. The game offers no explicit danger/safety communication beyond implicit board position analysis (piece adjacency = threat). Faction identity is purely color-based with animated character variations. World state is communicated through board saturation levels and piece distribution patterns. Regional differences (US vs JP versions) affected copyright graphics and console imagery but not core manifestation layer mechanics. Game designed for 2-4 players with CPU opponent option. NES version developed under tight constraints (six weeks, no development hardware/software per designer Graeme Devine).
sources_used: tcrf - Spot: The Video Game (NES) - The Cutting Room Floor
